2286356.title: Union Home Minister and Minister of Cooperation Shri Amit Shah inaugurates the first phase of the 'Museum of Word' in Kolkata
2286356.body_html:
Ministry of Home Affairs
Union Home Minister and Minister of Cooperation Shri Amit Shah inaugurates the first phase of the 'Museum of Word' in Kolkata
Today, this Museum of Words is giving concrete shape to Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi Ji's resolve for the cultural renaissance of the nation
This Museum of Words will serve as a powerful medium to carry the journey of India's languages, culture, and cultural renaissance to future generations
Language is shaped by words, words are shaped by emotions, and culture is shaped by language, without understanding this journey, the cultural renaissance of India cannot be complete
We have never shied away from innovation and have always embraced all that is noble and auspicious, that is why, despite countless challenges and assaults, our cultural journey stands as steadfast as the Himalayas
The Museum of Words should be expanded to encompass digital technologies and Artificial Intelligence, enabling Indian languages to evolve in line with the technologies of the future
This Museum of Words chronicles the entire journey of language—from Omkar to grammar, the Shruti and Smriti traditions, palm-leaf manuscripts, stone inscriptions, and the printing press, all the way to Artificial Intelligence
This museum is an innovative initiative to preserve India's multilingual consciousness, knowledge traditions, and collective memory through visual, auditory, and immersive experiences
This museum has accomplished the significant task of transforming the invisible word into a visual, auditory, tactile, and immersive experience
Bengal has long been a global beacon of India's cultural consciousness, time has now come for it to once again assume the role of guiding the world
Let us resolve to build a Viksit Bharat by 2047 and restore India's position as a Vishwa Guru, drawing strength from our words, our culture, and our self-confidence
Our resolve for language should be that every family becomes the first school of learning, every university emerges as a centre of original thought, every language is empowered with digital and AI capabil
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 7:36PM by PIB Delhi

Union Home Minister and Minister of Cooperation Shri Amit Shah today inaugurated the first phase of the 'Museum of Word' in Kolkata, the capital of West Bengal. On the occasion, West Bengal Chief Minister Shri Suvendu Adhikari, Finance Minister Shri Swapan Dasgupta, Union Home Secretary, Union Culture Secretary, Director Intelligence Bureau (IB), and several other distinguished dignitaries were present.

Addressing the gathering, Union Home Minister Shri Amit Shah said that today is a momentous day for the languages and culture of the entire nation. Today, this Museum of Words is giving concrete shape to Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi Ji's resolve for the cultural renaissance of the nation. Shri Shah said that the expression of the culture of any nation or region is impossible without language, and language itself cannot exist without words born out of human emotions and expression. He said that unless future generations understand that language is formed by words, words emerge from emotions, culture is shaped through language, and culture finds its expression through language, the cultural renaissance of the country cannot be achieved. Shri Shah expressed confidence that this Museum of Words would serve as a powerful medium for carrying the journey of India's languages, culture, and cultural renaissance to future generations.

Union Home Minister Shri Amit Shah said that the Museum of Word beautifully depicts the entire journey of the composition of Panini's grammar. He said that the Rigveda states that noble and auspicious thoughts should continue to come to us from all directions—thoughts that are never suppressed, never obstructed, and on the basis of which we should shape and advance our social life. Shri Shah said that narrow-mindedness can never arise in our civilization because there is no place for narrowness in the very foundation of Indian thought. He added that whenever we perform Bhoomi Pujan or recite the Shanti Mantra, we pray for peace not only for humanity but also for animals, plants, and the entire universe. Therefore, he said, narrow-mindedness can never be a symbol of Indian culture.

Shri Amit Shah said that the Museum of Word will educate future generations about India's languages and culture for generations to come, introduce them to the country's rich cultural heritage, and also guide them in the way of life rooted in that heritage. He said that India has never imposed restrictions on ideas or thought. Shri Shah noted that the museum traces the entire journey of the written word—from the sacred Omkar to the origin of words and the development of grammar; from the Shrutis and Smritis to manuscripts and inscriptions; and from the printing press to the modern era. Union Home Minister said that the Museum of Words should be expanded to encompass digital technologies and Artificial Intelligence (AI), enabling Indian languages to evolve in line with the technologies of the future. He said that India has never shied away from innovation and has always embraced all that is noble and auspicious. That is why, despite countless challenges and assaults, the nation's cultural journey stands as steadfast as the Himalayas. He further said that the Museum of Words is an innovative initiative to preserve India's multilingual consciousness, knowledge traditions, and collective memory through visual, auditory, and immersive experiences.

Union Home Minister said that West Bengal is home to many museums, and he is confident that the Museum of Word will establish a prominent place for itself in a very short time. He said that every seeker of language and words who visits the museum will certainly leave with valuable knowledge and inspiration. Shri Shah said that introducing future generations to the science of words and linguistics was an extremely subtle and challenging task, but the Ministry of Culture has accomplished it successfully. He remarked that while words themselves cannot be confined, the Museum of Word has successfully captured and presented the science behind them. He said that words can be heard, spoken, lived, and passed on to future generations, and the Museum of Word will play a vital role in preserving and transmitting this legacy. Shri Amit Shah further said that through the Museum of Word, the Ministry of Culture has transformed India's intangible heritage into an experience that is visible, audible, tangible, and immersive.

Union Home Minister said that when the Museum of Word poses the question of what the world's first spoken word might have been, and the answer Omkar appears, one is naturally inclined to feel that Omkar was indeed the world's first word. He said that listening to a mother's lullabies in one's own language instantly evokes memories of one's mother. Shri Shah added that the museum houses a wide range of artefacts and manuscripts that offer a tangible and immersive experience to visitors. He said that the museum preserves numerous dialects, languages, and the folk traditions of every region of the country, narrating India's cultural journey through the medium of words. It also showcases inscriptions, copper plates, Bhojpatra manuscripts, handwritten manuscripts, and a rich collection of printed books. Union Home Minister said that after the journey from digital text to audio archives, the next milestone is to take this legacy into the era of Artificial Intelligence (AI).

Shri Amit Shah said that every gallery of the Museum of Word has been designed in a highly scientific manner. He said that the orientation space begins with the origin of language and attempts to explain its evolution through myths, lullabies, and sensory experiences. Shri Shah said that every individual has a deep emotional bond with their mother tongue. He emphasized that this attachment to one's mother tongue should become an integral part of one's character.

Union Home Minister and Minister of Cooperation Shri Amit Shah said that linguistics is a science that has evolved naturally. He noted that while language is scientific in nature, it has developed in response to human needs. Languages have evolved organically over time, and it is this natural evolution that has led to their widespread acceptance in society. Shri Amit Shah said that numerous great personalities, poets, and writers have contributed to preserving and enriching every language of the country. To honour their invaluable contributions, a dedicated gallery has been created in the Museum of Word. Union Home Minister said that the future of the country does not depend on how crowded its colleges and universities are. Rather, it depends on how many young people and adolescents visit its libraries. He appealed to the youth to make libraries an integral part of shaping their personality and intellectual growth.

Union Home Minister Shri Amit Shah said that West Bengal has long been a global beacon of India's civilizational consciousness. He said that there was a prolonged period during which attempts were made to detach Bengal from India's cultural consciousness, and that narrow and foreign ideologies diminished Bengal's ability to contribute to the nation and the world. Shri Shah said that Bengal is the land of Maharshi Aurobindo, Swami Vivekananda, Ramakrishna Paramahansa, and Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. He added that Bengal has also produced some of the country's finest scientists, eminent litterateurs, and one of India's greatest revolutionaries, Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose. He noted that Bengal had traditionally remained decades ahead of the rest of the country, guiding the nation with its intellectual and cultural leadership. Expressing confidence in Bengal's future, Shri Amit Shah said that the intellectual beacon of India's civilizational consciousness would once again awaken the world with its full strength. He said that under the leadership of Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi and Shri Suvendu Adhikari, the ongoing transformation in Bengal gives him confidence that the state will soon re-emerge as a radiant beacon of Indian consciousness. Union Home Minister further said that India cannot be fully understood without studying Maharshi Aurobindo. He noted that Maharshi Aurobindo interpreted India's past, explained its present, and envisioned its future for the coming generations. Quoting Maharshi Aurobindo, Shri Shah said that one day Bharat Mata would undoubtedly reclaim her place as the Vishwa Guru and strive to offer her very best to humanity.

Shri Amit Shah urged the younger generation to understand the power of words and language, refine their pronunciation, and internalize the principles of grammar to make their expression more effective and impactful. He said that doing so would greatly enhance their acceptance, credibility, and influence in society.

Union Home Minister Shri Amit Shah said that the entire nation is celebrating the 150th anniversary of Vande Mataram, and it is particularly significant that the Museum of Word is being inaugurated at such a historic moment. He said that when one stands on the stage and listens to the complete rendition of Vande Mataram, one truly realizes the depth of devotion and reverence that every citizen should have for Bharat Mata. Shri Shah remarked that despite the profound message embodied in the song, the country remained unaware of its full significance for nearly 76–77 years after Independence. He expressed satisfaction that today, Vande Mataram is sung in its entirety in schools across the country, describing it as a matter of great joy

Shri Amit Shah said that Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi conferred Classical Language status on Bengali in 2024. He also noted that the nation recently commemorated the 125th birth anniversary of Dr. Syama Prasad Mookerjee. Shri Shah said that it was Dr. Syama Prasad Mookerjee who had invited Gurudev Rabindranath Tagore to deliver the convocation address in Bengali at the University of Calcutta. He noted that during British rule, this was the first time that a convocation address had been delivered in an Indian language at any university in the country. He said that today, Bengal is once again moving steadily towards becoming 'Sonar Bangla'.

Union Home Minister Shri Amit Shah said that while human beings often believe they created language, he believes that it is language that has shaped humanity. He said that a person truly becomes human when emotions are awakened, and it is language that awakens those emotions. The foundation of every language lies in words—especially the words of one's own language—and the Museum of Word will play an important role in preserving and promoting this legacy. Shri Shah said that five principles are crucial for the preservation and development of languages. First, the language spoken within the family should become a child's first school. Second, a child's education should begin in the mother's tongue. Third, schools and universities should become centres of original thinking, because only then can society make meaningful contributions to innovation and creativity. He said that universities should lead the way in fostering original thought. He also emphasized that every Indian language should be made digitally and AI-enabled, thereby extending its vitality and relevance for another thousand years. Union Home Minister said that libraries and museums should not remain confined to their physical buildings. Instead, efforts should be made to ensure that the values, knowledge, and emotions they embody reach the country's youth and adolescents through dedicated outreach campaigns. Shri Amit Shah further urged every young person to learn at least one other Indian language alongside their mother tongue. He said that this would help not only in preserving one's own mother tongue but also in ensuring the long-term vitality of all Indian languages. He added that if governments, the education system, and, above all, the people of the country embrace and promote these five principles, it would make a significant contribution to the preservation, promotion, and service of India's languages.

Shri Amit Shah said that from the soil of Bengal, regarded as a beacon of Indian culture, the nation should resolve to strengthen its culture through the power of words. He said that a strong culture will inspire self-confidence, and with that self-confidence, India will realize the vision of a 'Viksit Bharat' by 2047 and fulfill its aspiration of re-establishing Bharat Mata as the Vishwa Guru. Shri Shah said that the Museum of Word will serve as the foundation for realizing this vision.

***

2286342.title: Union Home Minister and Minister of Cooperation Shri Amit Shah chairs a review meeting on West Bengal’s law and order situation in Kolkata, today
2286342.body_html:
Ministry of Home Affairs
Union Home Minister and Minister of Cooperation Shri Amit Shah chairs a review meeting on West Bengal’s law and order situation in Kolkata, today
Union Home Minister Shri Amit Shah issues several key directives to strengthen the state's security apparatus, judicial system and police capabilities
'Women Outreach Programme' should be conducted prioritising the security of women in rural areas
Officials were directed to establish regular dialogue with rural women and strive to raise awareness and empower them
Emphasis was laid on taking necessary steps to establish an ANTF, ATS and 'Special Investigation Agency (SIA)'—modeled after the NIA—within the state
A decision was taken for the NIA to conduct a thorough review of all terrorism and UAPA-related cases concerning the state
Illegal encroachments on government land in border areas must be removed
Union Home Minister assures adequate budgetary support for modernization of the state police and improvement of service delivery systems
One-third of the security camps established in areas formerly affected by Left-Wing Extremism will be converted into public service centres to ensure the availability of government services
Capacity building in the field of cybersecurity should be strengthened, regular NCORD meetings should be held, and a comprehensive flood management plan should be prepared for Kolkata
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 7:02PM by PIB Delhi

Union Home Minister and Minister of Cooperation Shri Amit Shah chaired a review meeting on the West Bengal’s law and order situation in Kolkata, today. The meeting was attended by the Chief Minister of West Bengal, the State Chief Secretary, and senior officials from the Union Ministry of Home Affairs and the West Bengal government.

 

During the meeting, Union Home Minister Shri Amit Shah gave several important instructions to strengthen the state's security apparatus, judicial system and police capabilities. Prioritizing women's safety in rural areas, Union Home Minister directed the implementation of a 'Women Outreach Programme.' He emphasized the need to create awareness and empower rural women through regular interaction with officials. He also stated that the state government could directly contact the Union Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) should any need arise in this regard.

To further strengthen the state's internal security, emphasis was laid on taking necessary steps to establish an 'Anti-Narcotics Task Force (ANTF),' an 'Anti-Terrorist Squad (ATS),' and a 'Special Investigation Agency (SIA)'—modeled after the NIA—within the state. Additionally, it was decided that the National Investigation Agency (NIA) would conduct a thorough review of all terrorism and UAPA-related cases concerning the state. Directives were also issued to remove illegal encroachments on government land in the state's border areas.

 

 

Special emphasis was laid on capacity augmentation, infrastructure strengthening, and the development of state-of-the-art facilities for the West Bengal Police. Union Home Minister and Minister of Cooperation Shri Amit Shah assured that adequate budgetary support would be provided for the modernization of the state police and improvement of service delivery systems. In this process, a comprehensive review of the police force cadre will be conducted to increase its strength in line with the national average, and the number of police districts will be rationalized. Additionally, emphasis was placed on filling all vacant posts in a time-bound manner, reviewing training capacities, phasing out reliance on civic volunteers, and prioritizing the recruitment of constables.

A consensus was reached to convert one-third of the security camps established in areas formerly affected by Left-Wing Extremism into Public Service Centres to ensure the access to government services. Furthermore, directives were issued regarding capacity building in the cybersecurity sector, holding regular NCORD meetings to combat drug trafficking, and formulating a comprehensive flood management plan for Kolkata. At the end of the meeting, key priorities for immediate action were identified and the concerned agencies were directed to ensure their time-bound implementation.

2286326.title: CCPA initiates suo motu action against 41 restaurants for levying service charge by default; action based on consumer complaints received through National Consumer Helpline
2286326.ministry: Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food & Public Distribution
2286326.body_html:
Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food & Public Distribution
CCPA initiates suo motu action against 41 restaurants for levying service charge by default; action based on consumer complaints received through National Consumer Helpline
Action follows complaints supported by bills showing service charge was added without consumers' explicit consent
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 5:42PM by PIB Delhi

The Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) has initiated suo motu action against 41 restaurants across the country for violation of consumer rights and adoption of unfair trade practices by levying service charge by default on consumers' bills.

The action has been taken based on complaints received through the National Consumer Helpline (NCH). The complaints were supported by invoices showing that service charge had been automatically added to consumers' bills without obtaining their explicit consent.

Based on these complaints, CCPA conducted investigations and found that the automatic levy of service charge violated the Guidelines to Prevent Unfair Trade Practices and Protection of Consumer Interest with Regard to Levy of Service Charge in Hotels and Restaurants. The Authority also found that such practice amounts to an unfair trade practice under Section 2(47) of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019.

The Hon'ble High Court of Delhi, in its judgment dated 28 March 2025 in National Restaurant Association of India & Ors. v. Union of India & Anr., upheld the validity of the CCPA's Guidelines on Service Charge and held that mandatory collection of service charge is contrary to law. The Court further clarified that all restaurant establishments are required to comply with the Guidelines and that CCPA is free to enforce them in accordance with law.

Key provisions of the CCPA Guidelines

The Guidelines to Prevent Unfair Trade Practices and Protection of Consumer Interest with Regard to Levy of Service Charge in Hotels and Restaurants, issued on 4 July 2022, provide that:

  • No hotel or restaurant shall add service charge automatically or by default in the food bill.
  • No collection of service charge shall be done by any other name.
  • No hotel or restaurant shall force a consumer to pay service charge and must clearly inform consumers that it is voluntary, optional and entirely at the consumer's discretion.
  • No restriction on entry or provision of services shall be imposed on consumers for not paying service charge.
  • Service charge shall not be added to the food bill and GST shall not be levied on such amount.

 

Action taken by CCPA

In one such case, CCPA passed a final order against Chaayos (Sunshine Teahouse Pvt. Ltd.), imposing a penalty of ₹50,000 for levying service charge by default and directing the company to reimburse the service charge collected from the consumer.

The company has also been directed to modify its software-generated billing system across all its outlets to ensure that service charge or any similar charge is not added automatically to consumers' bills. CCPA has also passed final orders against the following restaurant establishments:

  1. Cafe Blue Bottle, Patna
  2. China Gate Restaurant Pvt. Ltd.
  3. Fiesta Barbeque Nation (Barbeque Nation Hospitality Private Limited)
  4. FOO Ahmedabad Restaurant (Pebble Street Hospitality Pvt. Ltd.)
  5. L'Opera French Bakery Private Limited
  6. Zorro – The Luxury Night Club (a unit of Rudra Hospitality Private Limited)
  7. Chaayos (Sunshine Teahouse Pvt. Ltd.)

Further proceedings are underway against other restaurants where complaints have been received and examined.

The Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) has initiated suo motu action against 41 restaurants for levying service charge by default, based on complaints received through the National Consumer Helpline (NCH).

Reaffirming that service charge is voluntary and entirely at… pic.twitter.com/bESSxvTX4U

— Pralhad Joshi (@JoshiPralhad) July 19, 2026

 

Consumers can report violation

CCPA encourages consumers to report instances where restaurants levy service charge by default through the National Consumer Helpline by calling 1915 (toll-free) or through the NCH platform. The Authority continues to closely monitor complaints relating to service charge and will take appropriate action against establishments found to be violating the Consumer Protection Act, 2019 and the Guidelines to Prevent Unfair Trade Practices and Protection of Consumer Interest with Regard to Levy of Service Charge in Hotels and Restaurants. CCPA remains committed to safeguarding consumer rights and ensuring fair, transparent and consumer-friendly business practices across the hospitality sector.

2286324.title: MoS Raksha Nikhil Khadse calls upon youth officers to become catalysts of nation-building
2286324.ministry: Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports
2286324.body_html:
Ministry of Youth Affairs and Sports
MoS Raksha Nikhil Khadse calls upon youth officers to become catalysts of nation-building
Fourth and Final Chintan Shivir concludes in Lucknow with roadmap for 'Viksit Yuva For Viksit Bharat'
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 5:35PM by PIB Delhi

The fourth Regional Chintan Shivir of the Department of Youth Affairs, Ministry of Youth Affairs & Sports, concluded today at Indira Gandhi Pratishthan, Lucknow, bringing to a close the nationwide series of regional consultations held under the theme "विकसित युवा बनाएगा विकसित भारत (Viksit Yuva for Viksit Bharat)." Centred on "Samadhaan Se Sankalp", the second day focused on converting the ideas and recommendations emerging from the previous day's deliberations into actionable strategies for strengthening youth-centric governance and grassroots implementation


Addressing the session, Smt. Raksha Nikhil Khadse, Union Minister of State of Youth Affairs & Sports,  said youth officers have a pivotal responsibility in shaping the future of the nation by inspiring young people to actively participate in nation-building. "Because you work on the ground, you understand challenges better, ask the right questions and find practical solutions. That commitment was clearly reflected in every presentation," she observed.

Appreciating the dedication of field functionaries, she said the presentations on MY Bharat, Experiential Learning Programme (ELP), Prime Minister Internship Scheme (PMIS) and Fit India reflected their grassroots commitment and practical understanding of local challenges. "This is not merely a duty; it is a responsibility. Along with building careers, our youth must also think about taking the country forward. They will write the future of India, and each one of you has the opportunity to become a hero for the youth," she said.

 

Appreciating the presentations made during the Chintan Shivir, the Minister of State said they reflected the dedication and hard work of officers working at the grassroots. She noted that initiatives such as MY Bharat, Experiential Learning Programme (ELP), Prime Minister Internship Scheme (PMIS) and Fit India had demonstrated how effective implementation at the field level leads to better policy outcomes.

Shri Shiv Ratan,  Joint Secretary, Department of Youth Affairs,  said the growing interest of various Ministries in partnering with MY Bharat reflects the platform's emergence as a key vehicle for youth engagement and nation-building. He urged officers to deepen collaboration with State Governments, educational institutions and local stakeholders, noting that stronger partnerships would further strengthen the Department's outreach and impact. "Every State has its own challenges and every challenge requires a State-specific solution. Our focus should be on strengthening the organisation at the grassroots by developing local solutions to local problems while remaining aligned with the national vision of Viksit Bharat." he said.

Addressing the officials , Dr. Priyanka Shukla, CEO, MY Bharat, appreciated the active participation of officers throughout the Chintan Shivir and encouraged them to adopt innovative approaches to youth engagement. She emphasised that the success of MY Bharat lies in empowering young people through meaningful opportunities for volunteerism, leadership and community participation, while ensuring that best practices from across States are effectively documented and scaled.

 

The second day commenced with technical sessions on the Prime Minister Internship Scheme (PMIS) and Fit India deriving collaborative efforts across departments. A major highlight of the concluding day was the presentation of thematic action plans and case studies developed by the working groups on priority areas of the Department. The groups presented recommendations on Nasha Mukt Bharat, Fit India, Gender Sensitisation, Youth Leadership and Volunteerism, Experiential Learning Programmes, community mobilisation, and institutional strengthening of MY Bharat and MY Bharat NSS.

The key takeaway from the two-day Chintan Shivir was the Department's collective resolve to further strengthen MY Bharat as a platform for youth-led nation-building through greater grassroots outreach, institutional convergence and collaborative governance. The deliberations underscored the importance of deeper community engagement, behavioural change communication, stronger partnerships with educational institutions and local stakeholders, technology-enabled programme delivery, and continuous capacity building of field functionaries.  Officials also emphasised the need for State-specific implementation strategies, regular sharing of best practices, and sustained dialogue between the Ministry and field officers to ensure more effective execution of flagship initiatives.

During the valedictory session, Uttar Pradesh was recognised as the best-performing State in terms of the highest number of MY Bharat registrations, while the Union Territory of Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu was acknowledged for achieving the highest registration percentage relative to its youth population, reflecting exemplary grassroots mobilisation and outreach.The recommendations emerging from the Chintan Shivir will guide future programme implementation and reinforce the Department of Youth Affairs' commitment to creating Viksit Yuva as the foundation of Viksit Bharat @2047.

*****

2286307.title: Meeting of Government with Leaders of Political Parties held today
2286307.body_html:
Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs
Meeting of Government with Leaders of Political Parties held today
58 Leaders from 40 Political Parties attended Meeting
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 4:20PM by PIB Delhi

A meeting has been held under the Chairmanship of Shri Rajnath Singh, Union Minister of Defence with Leaders of political parties today (19th July, 2026) in Parliament House Complex, New Delhi to discuss issues relating to ensuing Monsoon Session of Parliament, 2026. The meeting was called by Shri Kiren Rijiju, Union Minister of Parliamentary Affairs and Minority Affairs. The meeting was also attended by Union Minister for Health & Family Welfare and Chemicals & Fertilizers, Shri Jagat Prakash Nadda, who is also Leader of the House in Rajya Sabha, Minister of State (Independent Charge) of Law & Justice and Minister of State of Parliamentary Affairs, Shri Arjun Ram Meghwal and Minister of State of Parliamentary Affairs and Information and Broadcasting, Dr. L. Murugan. In total, the meeting was attended by 58 Leaders from 40 political parties including Ministers.

At the outset, Shri Rajnath Singh, Minister of Defence made introductory remarks and welcomed all the Leaders attending the meeting and thereafter, Minister of Parliamentary Affairs conducted the meeting. He informed the leaders that the Monsoon Session, 2026 of Parliament will commence on Monday, the 20th July, 2026 and subject to exigencies of Government Business, the session may conclude on Thursday, the 13th August, 2026. The Session will provide a total of 19 sittings spread over a period of 25 days.

   

Shri Rijiju further stated that Legislative Business for the ensuing Monsoon Session has already been published in Bulletin Part-II by both the Secretariats of Parliament on 16th July, 2026.

He requested all the Leaders for their cooperation for the smooth functioning of Parliament and stated that the Government is ready to discuss any other important issues on the floors of the Houses as permitted under the Rules of Procedure of both the Houses of Parliament. Leaders of different political parties expressed their views on various issues likely to be raised by them during the forthcoming Monsoon Session of Parliament and assured the Government to provide co-operation. The Minister of Parliamentary Affairs took note of all issues raised by the Leaders and thanked all the Leaders for attending the meeting, expressing their views and for their active and effective participation.

In last, Shri Rajnath Singh thanked all the leaders for attending the meeting, expressing their views and for their active and effective participation.

2286302.title: Dr Jitendra Singh Calls for Mass Public Movement Against Cancer, Diabetes and Obesity; Stresses Early Detection and Scientific Awareness
2286302.body_html:
Ministry of Science & Technology
Dr Jitendra Singh Calls for Mass Public Movement Against Cancer, Diabetes and Obesity; Stresses Early Detection and Scientific Awareness
Union Minister Urges early detection, right medical advice and evidence-based healthcare; says healthy citizens are essential for Viksit Bharat
Evidence-Based Health awareness Must Replace Misinformation in the Fight Against Lifestyle Diseases: Dr Jitendra Singh
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 3:55PM by PIB Delhi

Calling for a nationwide People's Movement (Jan Andolan) against cancer, diabetes, obesity and other lifestyle diseases, Union Minister of State (Independent Charge) for Science & Technology and Earth Sciences; Minister of State in the Prime Minister's Office, Department of Atomic Energy, Department of Space, Personnel, Public Grievances and Pensions, Dr. Jitendra Singh today said that India's response to the growing burden of Cancer, Diabetes, obesity and other non-communicable diseases must move beyond hospitals and become a mass public movement centred on early detection, right medical advice and evidence-based healthcare.

The Minister said the country today faces not merely a lack of awareness but also the challenge of widespread health misinformation, making scientific communication and evidence-based health communication more important than ever for realizing the vision of Viksit Bharat.

Dr. Jitendra Singh was addressing the launch of "UNBROKEN: My Battle with Cancer and the Will to Lead", authored by Cancer survivor, eminent Scientist and former Senior Adviser, Department of Science & Technology, Dr. Akhilesh Gupta. The event was attended by eminent medical professionals, academic and scientific fraternity, senior government officials, researchers and members of the scientific community, who came together to celebrate Dr. Gupta's remarkable journey of resilience after overcoming cancer.

Describing the book as much more than a personal memoir, Dr. Jitendra Singh said it chronicles an extraordinary journey of courage, resilience, integrity and unwavering faith in science. Having read the book in its entirety, he said it offers important lessons not only for patients battling serious illnesses but also for doctors, scientists, administrators and young professionals, demonstrating how determination and the right medical guidance can transform adversity into inspiration.

Referring to India's rapidly changing disease profile, Dr. Jitendra Singh said non-communicable diseases have emerged as one of the country's biggest public health challenges. Cancer, diabetes, obesity, fatty liver disease and other metabolic disorders are rising across all regions and are increasingly affecting younger populations. He said several cancers are now curable or manageable if diagnosed early, making timely screening and prompt medical intervention the most effective tools for reducing mortality and improving treatment outcomes.

Expressing concern over the growing prevalence of obesity and unhealthy lifestyles, the Minister said preventive healthcare must become a national habit rather than an individual choice. He stressed that balanced nutrition, regular physical activity and periodic health check-ups should form an integral part of daily life if India is to curb the growing burden of lifestyle diseases.

Dr. Jitendra Singh cautioned that the country today not only requires greater dissemination of health information but also a check on the alarming spread of misinformation relating to nutrition, diet and disease management. He said unverified advice and pseudoscientific claims often delay proper treatment and discourage patients from approaching qualified healthcare professionals in time. He called for greater scientific awareness so that citizens rely on evidence-based medical advice while making decisions relating to their health.

Highlighting the Government's efforts to strengthen healthcare through science and innovation, Dr. Jitendra Singh said India is increasingly developing Indian data for Indian solutions. Referring to the Genome India Programme, indigenous biotechnology innovations and affordable advanced therapies for diseases including cancer, he said the country's scientific ecosystem is steadily delivering world-class healthcare solutions suited to Indian conditions while making cutting-edge treatment more affordable and accessible.

The Minister said the Government has simultaneously expanded access to quality healthcare through initiatives such as Ayushman Bharat while strengthening indigenous biomedical research, biotechnology innovation and healthcare infrastructure. He noted that these efforts are helping India emerge as an important global destination for affordable healthcare innovation and biomanufacturing.

Calling for stronger collaboration between government, research institutions, academia, clinicians and industry, Dr. Jitendra Singh emphasized that translating scientific discoveries into affordable healthcare solutions requires greater public-private partnership. He said scientific research must ultimately improve the quality of life of ordinary citizens and contribute to a healthier society.

Referring once again to Dr. Akhilesh Gupta's journey, the Minister said the book demonstrates that resilience, self-belief, timely medical care and trust in science can overcome even the most formidable health challenges. He added that the author's experience also reinforces the importance of seeking treatment from qualified medical experts without losing valuable time to unscientific practices.

Concluding his address, Dr. Jitendra Singh said the vision of Viksit Bharat @2047 cannot be realized without a healthy population. He called upon every citizen to become an active participant in a nationwide People's Movement against cancer, diabetes, obesity and lifestyle diseases by adopting healthy lifestyles, encouraging regular health screening, promoting scientific awareness and helping eliminate health misinformation. Preventing disease, he said, is not the responsibility of governments or doctors alone but a shared national responsibility requiring collective participation from every section of society.

2286292.title: Odisha’s New Coastal Highway: A Gateway to Spiritual Journeys and Coastal Tourism Revolution
2286292.body_html:
Ministry of Road Transport & Highways
Odisha’s New Coastal Highway: A Gateway to Spiritual Journeys and Coastal Tourism Revolution
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 2:51PM by PIB Delhi

Odisha’s coastline is set to witness a major transformation with the construction of the new Rameshwar–Paradeep Coastal Highway, a 160.18 km corridor that will redefine connectivity across the state’s coastal belt. The project will be developed by the Ministry of Road Transport & Highways (MoRTH) with a total capital cost of ₹8,300.79 crore. The highway will pass through Khurda, Puri, Kendrapada and Jagatsinghpur districts, creating a seamless connection between important cultural, religious, tourism and economic destinations.

Designed to provide faster, safer and smoother travel, the project includes a 4-lane access-controlled highway from Rameshwar to Konark and a 2-lane highway with paved shoulders from Konark to Paradeep, with a design speed of 100 km/hour. Once completed, the corridor is expected to reduce travel time between Rameshwar and Paradeep by nearly 2 hours and 30 minutes, improving passenger movement as well as freight connectivity.

 

Connecting Odisha’s Spiritual Heritage

The new coastal highway will become a major catalyst for religious and cultural tourism by creating faster access to some of Odisha’s most iconic spiritual destinations.

The corridor will strengthen connectivity between the world-renowned Jagannath Temple in Puri, the historic Konark Sun Temple, and the revered Ramchandi Temple, making pilgrimage journeys more convenient for millions of devotees visiting Odisha every year.

Improved road infrastructure will not only reduce travel time but also enhance the overall pilgrimage experience by providing safer and more comfortable movement for tourists and devotees. The upcoming airport infrastructure in the region will further strengthen Odisha’s position as a major spiritual tourism destination, enabling easier access for visitors from across India and the world.

Beyond transportation, the highway represents how modern infrastructure can support the preservation and promotion of India’s cultural heritage by connecting people with historic temples, traditions and spiritual centres.

 

From Puri to Paradeep: Odisha’s New Tourism Highway

The Rameshwar–Paradeep corridor is set to become a new coastal tourism gateway, linking beaches, heritage sites, biodiversity hotspots and local communities along Odisha’s eastern coastline.

With improved connectivity between Puri, Konark and Paradeep, these destinations will emerge as part of a unified tourism circuit offering travellers a complete coastal experience — from spiritual heritage and ancient architecture to beaches and natural ecosystems.

The highway will open new opportunities for beach tourism, eco-tourism and nature-based travel, while providing easier access to important ecological destinations, including wildlife areas and the famous Gahirmatha turtle nesting sites.

Better connectivity will also encourage sustainable tourism practices by increasing awareness about Odisha’s unique coastal biodiversity and conservation efforts. Visitors will have greater access to natural landscapes while supporting responsible tourism models that protect fragile ecosystems.

 

Boost to Local Economy and Coastal Development

The new highway will create opportunities for local communities by encouraging the growth of hotels, homestays, restaurants, handicraft markets and tourism-based businesses along the corridor.

Aligned with the principles of PM GatiShakti, the project will connect 9 economic nodes and 5 logistics nodes, further strengthening Odisha’s economic network. It will also improve access to important hubs such as Paradeep Port, Puri Railway Station, Puri Airport and other regional economic centres.

2286291.title: From Banana Farms to Makhana Markets: The Highway Set to Power Bihar’s Agricultural Heartland
2286291.body_html:
Ministry of Road Transport & Highways
From Banana Farms to Makhana Markets: The Highway Set to Power Bihar’s Agricultural Heartland
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 2:50PM by PIB Delhi

Before dawn breaks over the fertile plains of Bihar, trucks loaded with bunches of bananas leave the farms of Khagaria, while sacks of makhana begin their journey from ponds and processing centres of Purnea, one of the country’s biggest makhana hubs. For years, traffic congestion and seasonal disruptions have often slowed the journey of these highly-valued crops. For a crop as perishable as banana, where every hour matters, faster and more reliable connectivity can help minimise spoilage, preserve quality and improve market realisations for farmers. The proposed upgradation of the Khagaria–Purnea section of NH-31 and NH-231 to a four-lane highway by the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways, recently approved by Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (CCEA), seeks to address these longstanding challenges.

The upgradation of the Khagaria-Purnea Section of NH-31 and NH-231 to 4-lane standard in Bihar at a cost of Rs.3936.05 crore is more of an investment in the people whose livelihoods depend on getting Bihar’s bananas and makhana to market efficiently, helping strengthen the economic backbone of the state’s agricultural heartland. In a region where a few hours can make a difference to the quality and value of agricultural produce, the upgradation of highways is poised to become more than a transport project.

 

Building Flood-Resilient Infrastructure in Bihar

Eastern Bihar’s transport network has historically been vulnerable to flooding, particularly in areas influenced by the Kosi and Ganga river systems. Seasonal flooding often affects road connectivity, slowing the movement of people and goods and increasing travel uncertainty for local communities.

The four-laning of the Khagaria–Purnea section is expected to incorporate engineering interventions aimed at enhancing the highway’s resilience against recurring flood-related challenges. By improving road geometry, strengthening pavement structures and integrating modern drainage systems, the project is designed to support more reliable transportation throughout the year.

 

Connecting Seemanchal to Opportunity

The Seemanchal region has long faced developmental challenges linked to limited connectivity. Districts such as Khagaria, Katihar and neighbouring areas often experience longer travel times to administrative, educational and economic hubs compared to many other parts of the state.

The upgraded corridor holds the potential to significantly improve regional mobility by reducing travel time between key destinations. Journeys that currently take around 3.5 to 4 hours are expected to be completed in approximately 1.5 to 2 hours once the corridor becomes operational.

 

Strengthening Eastern India’s Interstate Links

Beyond its importance for Bihar, the Khagaria–Purnea corridor holds strategic value for interstate connectivity across eastern India.

The upgraded highway will strengthen road links between Bihar and neighbouring West Bengal while improving access to Jharkhand through the broader National Highway network.

 

Tackling Traffic Chaos

The proposed upgradation of the Khagaria-Purnea section of NH-31 and NH-231 addresses longstanding bottlenecks, including insufficient geometric features, sharp curves and congestion through densely populated urban and semi-urban stretches across Khagaria, Bhagalpur, Katihar and Purnia districts. The Khagaria-Purnea project will upgrade the critical two-lane corridor to four lanes, strengthening connectivity across Bihar’s Seemanchal region and linking Khagaria, Bhagalpur, Katihar and Purnea with Patna, West Bengal and Jharkhand.

 

Linking Key Economic and Logistics Hubs

The upgraded corridor will strengthen connectivity to several of Bihar’s key economic, social and logistics hubs. By linking five PM Gati Shakti Economic Nodes, including one textile cluster, two mega food parks and two fishing and seafood parks, along with 11 logistics nodes comprising four major railway stations, one airport, four National Highways and two State Highways, the project will significantly enhance multimodal integration across the region.

2286279.title: Indian Team won 1 Gold and 3 Silver Medals at the 37th International Biology Olympiad 2026 in Vilnius, Lithuania
2286279.body_html:
Department of Atomic Energy
Indian Team won 1 Gold and 3 Silver Medals at the 37th International Biology Olympiad 2026 in Vilnius, Lithuania
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 1:42PM by PIB Mumbai

 

Mumbai, 19th July 2026

India performed very well at the 37th International Biology Olympiad (IBO) 2026 held in Vilnius, Lithuania from 12 July to 19 July 2026, with all 4 students winning medals – 1 Gold and 3 Silver.

The medallists were:

Bhavyaa Gunwal (GOLD) from Mahendragarh, Haryana

Soumil Maity (SILVER) from Howrah, West Bengal

Nishit Kalani (SILVER) from Pali, Rajasthan

Anmol Kumar (SILVER) from Mansa, Punjab

The team was accompanied by two leaders: Prof. Rekha Vartak (Retd. HBCSE-TIFR, Mumbai) and Dr. Anupama Ronad (HBCSE-TIFR), and two Scientific Observers: Dr. Ranjithsinh Devkar (M. S. University, Baroda) and Dr. Siddhesh Ghag (UM-DAE Centre of Excellence in Basic Sciences, Mumabi).

There were 307 students from 78 countries in this year's IBO. In all 31 gold, 61 silver, and 91 bronze medals were awarded.

The IBO 2026 examinations pushed the boundaries of secondary school biology through grueling exams expertly designed by the Vilnius University Life Sciences Centre. The practical examinations, lasting six hours in total, required students to tackle complex, hands-on experimental tasks across four highly specialized domains. In the Molecular Biology & Biochemistry lab, students performed restriction digestion and agarose gel analysis of plasmids alongside a biochemical enzyme assay. The Animal Physiology lab had them evaluate the pharmacokinetics of aspirin and quantify breakdown of the product in simulated blood samples using an in-house developed app and light box colorimeter. The Animal Morphology & Systematics lab required competitors to analyze the systematics of insect specimens for classification, which included precise mouth-part dissections. Finally, the Plant Computational Biology lab introduced cutting-edge digital analysis, tasking students with utilizing ImageJ software to evaluate plant root lengths, perform high-dimensionality macrophenotype analysis of distinct plant ecotypes, and conduct complex transcriptome analysis.

Complementing the practical section was an equally intense six-hour theoretical examination divided into two separate three-hour sessions. The theory portion featured 100 highly challenging test items that relied heavily on real-world research data and field study analysis. Rather than testing simple recall, these questions evaluated advanced problem-solving skills across the core biological sciences, including Cell & Molecular Biology, Plant & Animal Sciences, Genetics & Evolution, Ecology, Ethology, and Biosystematics.

We congratulate all the Indian students, and the rest of the Indian delegation for this excellent performance. The teachers and mentors from across the country and the Biology Olympiad cell of HBCSE deserve appreciation for training the students during the orientation and pre-departure camps at HBCSE. We thank the National Steering Committee, all the teacher organisations and the funding agencies of the Govt. of India for their continued strong support of the Olympiad programme.

This was India's 26th appearance at the IBO. Of the 104 students so far, 17 have received gold medals, 69 silver, 17 bronze, and 1 honourable mentions. In the last ten years, the gold and silver strike rates have been 25% and 68%, respectively.
 

***

2286278.title: All 4 students from Team India won Gold Medals at the 58th International Chemistry Olympiad, Tashkent, Uzbekistan
2286278.body_html:
Department of Atomic Energy
All 4 students from Team India won Gold Medals at the 58th International Chemistry Olympiad, Tashkent, Uzbekistan
Posted On: 19 JUL 2026 1:35PM by PIB Mumbai


Mumbai, 19th July 2026
India performed exceptionally well at the 58th International Chemistry Olympiad (IChO) 2026 held in Tashkent, Uzbekistan from 10 to 19 July 2026, with all four students bagging gold medals!

The students were:
Debadatta Priyadarshi (GOLD) from Bhubaneshwar, Odisha
Harshit Singla (GOLD) from Mandi Gobindgarh, Punjab
Kabeer Chillar (GOLD) from Delhi
Sandeep Kuchi GOLD) from Hyderabad, Telangana

There were four Jury Members from India: Prof. Subhajit Bandyopadhyay as Head Mentor, Dr. Indrani Sen (HBCSE, Mumbai) as Mentor, Dr. Anubendu Adhikary (IIT Kharagpur) and Dr. Jayasree Gopalakrishnan (NES Ratnam College of Arts, Science and Commerce, Mumbai) as Scientific Observers.

In the country-wise medals tally, India was placed at the first position, jointly with China, Vietnam, and Individual Participants Group B (from Russia). This year's IChO had 363 students from 93 countries, making it the biggest IChO till date. This is India's best performance in IChO with the first-ever all-gold haul!
The competition had both experimental and theoretical components.
The theoretical exam had 9 problems on identifying composition of a blue solution made from plant extracts and a yellow stone from a coal deposit (apparently also found in Avicenna's lab); kinetics of oscillatory BZ reaction; making porous framework materials (called COFs) using different functionalized organic compounds; estimating the mass of Uranium used in historic 1966 detonation to put off the natural gas fire in Urtabulak (after a blaze of 3 years); decoding structure of a chiral cardiolipin (a kind of phosphoester, part of intracellular membranes) having four identical fatty acid residues; using novel carbon allotropes —cyclocarbons— to form nanosized molecular belt by stabilizing it with a macrocyclic metal complex; nitrogen fixation in ammonia production plants using transition metal catalysts; catalytic reduction of atmospheric CO2 along with use of photosensitizers for enhanced efficiency; and reactions of synthetic analogues of cyclodextrins, testing students on stereochemical and regio-specific transformations.

The experimental exam consisted of 3 problems involving (i) synthesis of a metal complex using given (unknown reagents) and analysis of its composition by qualitative analysis and complexometric titration; (ii) identifying 20 given mixtures of biochemicals (such as glucose, urea, uric acid, cholestrol's ester and a oil) by using given 5 enzyme mixtures through microscale tests; and (iii) identifying compositions of 3 acid mixtures through pH-metric titrations followed by analysis of two indicator solutions (one natural indicator from red cabbage and another mixture of two indicators) through pH-metry.
We congratulate the students and the rest of the delegation for their excellent performance! The Chemistry Olympiad cell of HBCSE and the resource pool of external teachers and mentors deserve appreciation for preparing the team well during the orientation and pre-departure camps held at HBCSE.

This was the 27th appearance of India at the IChO. In all these years, 32% of our students have got gold medals, 51% silver, and 17% bronze. In the last ten appearances, the percentages of gold and silver medals have been 43% and 53%, respectively.

We thank the National Steering Committee on Science Olympiads, teacher organisations and the funding agencies of the Govt. of India (DAE, DST and MoE) for their continued strong support of the Olympiad programme which enables us to showcase Indian talent on the international arena.
